Press enter or click to view image in full size

7 min read
2 hours ago

--

This isn’t about writing better code. It’s about seeing code in a way you never could before.

I want to tell you something that took me five years to figure out.
Not because I wasn’t paying attention. Not because I was lazy. But because nobody told me there was an entire dimension of software development I was completely blind to.
Here it is:
Building a feature and securing a feature are two completely different skills. And most developers — including really good ones — only ever learn one of them.
The moment I understood this, everything changed. And the reason I finally understood it wasn’t a course, wasn’t a book, wasn’t a senior developer mentoring me.
It was AI.
Let me show you exactly what I mean.

The Dimension You Can’t See
When you build software, your brain works in one direction.
Forward.
You think about what the user is supposed to do. You design the happy path. You handle the obvious edge cases. You make sure the feature works exactly as intended.
That’s development thinking. And it’s powerful. It’s what turns ideas into products.
But there’s another direction. A completely opposite one.
Adversarial thinking.
This is how attackers see your code. They’re not asking “does this work?” They’re asking “how can I make this do something it was never supposed to do?”
Same code. Completely different lens.
Security specialists spend years developing this lens. It’s a separate discipline. A separate career. And for most full stack developers juggling React, Node.js, databases, DevOps, and everything else — there’s simply no time to develop it from scratch.
Until now.
Because AI gives you that lens instantly. On demand. For any piece of code you write.

What This Actually Looks Like — A Real Story
Last year I was building a document management SaaS. File uploads, PDF viewing, team permissions. Standard stuff.
I wrote this endpoint:
app.post('/upload', auth, async (req, res) => {
const { filename } = req.body;
const uploadPath = path.join(__dirname, 'uploads', filename); fs.writeFileSync(uploadPath, req.file.buffer);
res.json({ success: true, path: uploadPath });
});
Clean. Simple. Works perfectly. I was happy with it.
Then I did something I’d never done before — I asked an AI to look at it from an attacker’s perspective.
The response didn’t say “this is wrong.” It said:
“Here’s what an attacker sees.”
And then it listed four things.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

What the attacker sees — #1
That filename comes directly from the request body. Send filename: "../../config/.env" and you can overwrite the server's environment file. This is directory traversal. It's not a complex hack. It's one line of crafted input.
What the attacker sees — #2
No file type validation. You’re thinking “users will upload PDFs.” The attacker is thinking “I’ll upload a script and see if I can execute it.”
What the attacker sees — #3
The response returns uploadPath — the actual internal server path. To you that's helpful. To an attacker that's a map of where everything lives.
What the attacker sees — #4
No file size limit. Send a 50GB file. Do it 100 times simultaneously. Watch the server die. That’s a denial of service and it costs the attacker nothing.

I sat with that for a minute.
Not because I felt bad about the code. But because I realized: I had been writing code for five years and I had never once thought about a single one of those four things.
Not because I’m a bad developer. Because development thinking doesn’t go there. It literally points in the opposite direction.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Fixed Version — Same Skill, Different Perspective
Here’s what the same endpoint looks like when written with adversarial thinking:
const path = require('path');
const crypto = require('crypto');
const multer = require('multer');const upload = multer({
limits: { fileSize: 10 * 1024 * 1024 }, // 10MB max — attacker can't crash with huge files
fileFilter: (req, file, cb) => {
const allowedTypes = ['application/pdf', 'image/jpeg', 'image/png'];
allowedTypes.includes(file.mimetype) ? cb(null, true) : cb(new Error('Invalid file type'), false);
}
});app.post('/upload', auth, upload.single('file'), async (req, res) => {
// Never use user-provided filenames — generate your own
const safeFilename = crypto.randomUUID() + path.extname(req.file.originalname); // Verify path stays within uploads directory
const uploadDir = path.resolve(__dirname, 'uploads');
const uploadPath = path.resolve(uploadDir, safeFilename);
if (!uploadPath.startsWith(uploadDir)) {
return res.status(400).json({ error: 'Invalid path' });
}
fs.writeFileSync(uploadPath, req.file.buffer);
// Return only what the client needs — never internal structure
res.json({ success: true, fileId: safeFilename });
});
The code isn’t harder. The developer isn’t more skilled.
The only thing that changed is the perspective it was written from.
That’s the entire point.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Second Story — Auth Flow Secrets
A few months later. Same project. Building authentication.
Five years in. I had built auth flows many times. I was comfortable.
I reviewed each piece as I built it — shifting into adversarial mode at each step.
Here’s what surfaced. Not mistakes. Attack vectors I hadn’t considered.

Attack Vector 1 — Your Reset Tokens Are a Skeleton Key
I stored password reset tokens as plain strings in the database. Functional. Clean. Completely standard in dozens of tutorials.
Adversarial perspective: if your database is ever compromised — through any breach, any leak, any vulnerability anywhere in your stack — those plain tokens let an attacker reset every single user’s password.
// Store only the hash — send the plain token to the user's email
const resetToken = crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex');
user.passwordResetToken = crypto
.createHash('sha256')
.update(resetToken)
.digest('hex');
// The plain resetToken goes in the email link
// The hash goes in the database
// Attacker gets the database — gets nothing useful
Attack Vector 2 — Your Error Messages Are Leaking User Data
This one is subtle and brilliant in its simplicity.
// This tells attackers which emails exist in your system
if (!user) return res.status(404).json({ message: 'User not found' });
if (!validPassword) return res.status(401).json({ message: 'Wrong password' });Two different error messages. Two different status codes. An attacker just needs to write a script that fires email addresses at your login endpoint and reads which response comes back. Now they have a list of every email registered in your app.
// This gives away nothing
if (!user || !validPassword) {
return res.status(401).json({ message: 'Invalid credentials' });
}One message. Always. Regardless of what failed.

Attack Vector 3 — The 30 Second Fix That Closes 11 Attack Surfaces
No HTTP security headers on my Express app. Invisible from a development perspective — your app works fine without them. From an adversarial perspective — multiple open attack surfaces.
const helmet = require('helmet');
app.use(helmet());One line. 11 security headers added automatically. Done.

Why This Changes Everything Going Forward
Here’s the thing about adversarial thinking once you start developing it:
You can’t turn it off.
I now catch myself mid-build asking questions I never asked before:

• “What happens if someone sends this field as an array instead of a string?”

• “What does this error message reveal about my database structure?”

• “What’s the most destructive thing someone could do with this endpoint?”
Those questions cost me 30 seconds. The AI review that answers them costs me 2 minutes.
The result is code that’s been seen from both directions before it ships.
Not because I became a security expert. Because I now have access to a second perspective that was always there — I just couldn’t see it before.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Prompt That Changed My Process
If you want to start building adversarial thinking today, use this exact prompt:
Look at this code from an attacker's perspective.
Don't tell me what's wrong with it as code.
Tell me what an attacker would attempt to do with it
and what they would succeed at.That reframe is everything.
You’re not asking for criticism. You’re asking for intelligence.
And the difference between those two things is the difference between feeling defensive about your code and feeling curious about your threat surface.

The Real Shift
Three years ago, the industry separated developers and security teams because the two disciplines seemed incompatible.
Developers build. Security teams break. Two different mindsets, two different departments, two different worlds.
AI is collapsing that separation.
Not by turning developers into security experts. But by making adversarial thinking accessible at the moment you’re writing code — not weeks later when a security team gets around to reviewing it.
The developers who figure this out early don’t just write more secure code.
They think differently about everything they build.
And in a world where every app handles real user data, real payments, and real trust — that difference is everything.

I’m Raj Namdev — Full Stack Developer with 5+ years of experience. I write about AI tools, code security, and the shifts that change how you think about building. Follow for one new perspective every week.
